proposals.title: Allosteric and multi-molecular mechanisms underlying the flickering blockade of presynaptic calcium channels
proposals.motivation: Presynaptic calcium channels are crucial for synaptic vesicle release, and their blockade by antagonists is a fundamental pharmacological tool. The observation of 'flickering' block, where channel conductance rapidly switches, is often interpreted as a simple bimolecular reaction. However, this model fails to explain complex kinetic patterns observed in high-resolution recordings, suggesting more intricate mechanisms may govern drug-channel interactions at the synapse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the flickering blockade of presynaptic CaV2 channels involves either cooperative binding of multiple drug molecules or allosteric modulation of the channel by a single bound antagonist, moving beyond the classic one-site, one-ligand model.
proposals.method: We will employ cell-attached and perforated-patch clamp recordings from calyx of Held terminals, focusing on CaV2.1 (P/Q-type) channels. High-temporal-resolution recordings of single-channel currents during application of ω-agatoxin IVA or ω-conotoxin MVIIC will be analyzed using hidden Markov modeling (HMM) with custom-developed multi-state kinetic schemes. These models will test for sequential binding steps and allosteric transitions. Experiments will be combined with rapid solution exchange to probe state-dependent binding kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel kinetic framework for understanding presynaptic calcium channel pharmacology, directly linking molecular blockade mechanisms to short-term plasticity. It will challenge the prevailing bimolecular assumption and offer a more accurate model for predicting how antagonists modulate synaptic strength and vesicle release dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While Neher's recent work has detailed sequential priming of vesicles, applying similar multi-step kinetic analysis to the drug blockade of the calcium channels that trigger release is novel. It directly tests whether synaptic channel pharmacology is as complex as the vesicle release machinery it controls.

proposals.title: Asymmetric Membrane Positioning of the Voltage Sensor Underlies the Voltage Dependence of Vesicle Priming
proposals.motivation: The voltage dependence of synaptic vesicle priming and release is a fundamental property of presynaptic terminals, crucial for short-term plasticity and information processing. While the voltage sensor is known to be intrinsic to the vesicle-associated protein machinery, its precise structural location within the membrane remains ambiguous. Determining whether this sensor is centrally located or asymmetrically positioned is key to understanding the biophysical mechanism coupling membrane potential to priming probability.
proposals.hypothesis: The voltage sensor responsible for the voltage dependence of vesicle priming is not centrally located within the lipid bilayer but is positioned asymmetrically, nearer to the cytoplasmic membrane surface. This asymmetric placement alters the local electric field experienced by the sensor, thereby defining its unique voltage sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will combine high-resolution electrophysiology with computational modeling. Experimentally, we will perform paired recordings from cultured neocortical neurons, using a step-protocol to isolate the voltage-dependent component of vesicle priming. We will manipulate the transmembrane electric field asymmetry using non-permeant ionic substitutions and membrane-targeted charged quenchers. Computationally, we will develop a modified sequential two-step priming model incorporating an asymmetric energy profile for the voltage-sensing step, fitting it to the experimental data on synaptic strength and short-term plasticity diversity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined biophysical model of the voltage-sensing step in vesicle priming, moving beyond a simple central-barrier assumption. It will directly link molecular sensor architecture to functional presynaptic diversity, offering a mechanistic explanation for how different synapses can exhibit distinct voltage sensitivities in their release machinery.
proposals.novelty: While voltage dependence is well-characterized phenomenologically, directly testing the physical asymmetry of the sensor's membrane position is a novel approach. It challenges the default assumption of a symmetrical energy barrier and integrates concepts from ion channel biophysics into the study of vesicle priming dynamics.

proposals.title: Structural Elucidation of the QX-314 Blocking Site in Voltage-Gated Sodium Channels Using Cryo-Electron Microscopy
proposals.motivation: QX-314 and related quaternary ammonium compounds are potent intracellular blockers of voltage-gated sodium (Nav) channels, crucial for understanding local anesthetic action and designing novel neuropharmacological tools. Despite decades of functional studies, the precise molecular architecture of the blocking site within the channel's inner pore remains unresolved. Determining this structure is essential for a mechanistic understanding of use-dependent block and for rational drug design targeting specific Nav channel isoforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the QX-314 blocking site is a defined pocket within the intracellular vestibule of the Nav channel, formed by specific residues from the S6 helices of domains I, III, and IV, and that its precise conformation explains the compound's voltage- and use-dependence.
proposals.method: We will employ single-particle cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) to determine high-resolution structures of a mammalian Nav channel (e.g., Nav1.4 or Nav1.7) in complex with QX-314. Channels will be purified in nanodiscs to maintain a native-like lipid environment. We will trap the blocked state using a two-step priming scheme: first, channels will be stabilized in an inactivated-like state using established protocols, then perfused with QX-314. Data collection will be performed on a 300 keV Titan Krios microscope, and structures will be solved using RELION and cryoSPARC, with focused refinement on the pore domain to resolve the drug density.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomic-resolution visualization of a clinically relevant local anesthetic bound to its intracellular site in a Nav channel. It will directly test long-standing models of the 'hydrophobic pathway' and 'modulated receptor' hypotheses. The structural data will serve as a blueprint for developing new, isoform-selective channel blockers with tailored kinetic properties.
proposals.novelty: While cryo-EM structures of Nav channels are available, none have captured a quaternary ammonium blocker like QX-314 in situ. The proposed approach of functionally priming the channel to a specific gating state before structural analysis integrates Neher's expertise in precise kinetic dissection with cutting-edge structural biology to solve a classic pharmacological problem.

proposals.title: Differential Block of Presynaptic Voltage-Gated Sodium Channels by QX Compounds Reveals Distinct Access Pathways for Vesicle Priming and Fusion
proposals.motivation: The precise spatiotemporal control of action potential-driven calcium influx is fundamental for vesicle release dynamics and synaptic plasticity. While QX compounds are established intracellular sodium channel blockers, their differential effects when applied intra- vs. extracellularly remain underexplored as a tool to dissect the membrane topology of release machinery access. Understanding this access is critical for models of priming and fusion steps, especially in light of recent findings on heterogeneous vesicle pools with distinct priming kinetics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intracellular QX application will preferentially inhibit a late, tightly membrane-coupled step in vesicle priming or fusion-pore opening, while extracellular application will block action potential propagation, allowing us to pharmacologically separate the voltage-sensing requirements of the vesicle release machinery from those of the presynaptic spike.
proposals.method: We will perform paired whole-cell recordings from connected layer 5 neocortical neurons in rat brain slices. Using a dual-patch configuration on the presynaptic neuron, we will apply QX-314 intracellularly via the recording pipette or QX-222 extracellularly in the bath. We will quantify synaptic transmission (EPSCs) while employing a well-established two-pulse protocol to assay vesicle pool dynamics and priming. The differential effects on initial release probability, paired-pulse ratio, and the slow recovery of the 'fully-primed' pool described in Neher's recent work will be analyzed to assign block to specific kinetic steps in the priming-fusion sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct pharmacological dissection of the voltage-dependent steps immediately preceding vesicle fusion, beyond action potential generation. It will test whether the 'mover' step for primed vesicles requires sodium channel conformational changes accessible only from the intracellular side. The findings will constrain biophysical models of the final trigger for release, linking channel topology to the diversity of synaptic strength and short-term plasticity.
proposals.novelty: While QX compounds are classic tools, their systematic side-specific application to probe the voltage-sensing requirements of the release machinery itself, rather than excitability, is novel. This approach directly tests the hypothesis that a late priming/fusion step is coupled to a sodium channel domain inaccessible to extracellular blockers.

proposals.title: Open-Channel Block as a Determinant of Synaptic Current Waveform and Short-Term Plasticity at the Neuromuscular Junction
proposals.motivation: The shape of the macroscopic end-plate current (EPC) is a fundamental determinant of synaptic efficacy and temporal precision. While vesicle release dynamics and postsynaptic receptor properties are well-studied, the impact of open-channel block (OCB) by endogenous molecules or drugs on the ensemble synaptic current remains poorly quantified. Understanding this modulation is crucial for interpreting synaptic strength and plasticity, especially in the context of Neher's recent work on priming and calcium buffering that shapes presynaptic output.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that open-channel block by compounds like QX-222 or endogenous polyamines significantly truncates the decay phase of the EPC, and that this effect interacts with presynaptic release probability and short-term plasticity to shape synaptic transmission.
proposals.method: We will perform two-electrode voltage-clamp recordings of macroscopic EPCs from frog (*Rana pipiens*) cutaneous pectoris nerve-muscle preparations. Following established protocols, we will bath-apply known open-channel blockers (e.g., QX-222) at varying concentrations. To dissect the interaction with presynaptic dynamics, we will measure EPCs during paired-pulse and short train stimulation under control and blocked conditions. Analysis will involve fitting EPC decays with multi-exponential functions and using a sequential two-step priming model, informed by Neher's recent work, to simulate how OCB-altered postsynaptic currents feed back on estimates of presynaptic parameters like vesicle pool size and release probability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for how open-channel block sculpts synaptic currents, directly linking a specific postsynaptic conductance mechanism to the measured parameters of synaptic strength. It will clarify how pharmacological or endogenous OCB can confound interpretations of presynaptic function and offer new insights into the synergy between pre- and postsynaptic determinants of short-term plasticity.
proposals.novelty: While OCB is a known biophysical phenomenon, its systematic investigation as a dynamic shaper of ensemble synaptic currents, integrated with modern models of presynaptic vesicle priming and short-term plasticity, represents a novel synthesis at a classic model synapse.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Permeant Ion-Like Properties of Synaptic Channel Blockers: A Kinetic Model for Modulation of Vesicle Priming and Release
proposals.motivation: Many drugs and endogenous modulators act as open-channel blockers of postsynaptic receptors, but their effects on presynaptic release machinery, particularly vesicle priming and fusion, are poorly quantified. A quantitative framework is needed to model how such blockers, by acting as slow permeant ions, influence presynaptic calcium dynamics and the sequential priming steps that determine synaptic strength and short-term plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating a channel blocker as a slow permeant ion competing with Ca²⁺ for pore transit will generate a predictive kinetic model. This model will show that blocker flux directly modulates the local [Ca²⁺] microdomain at the release site, thereby altering the occupancy of the sequential priming states of synaptic vesicles.
proposals.method: We will develop a kinetic model of a calcium-permeable channel (e.g., NMDA receptor or voltage-gated calcium channel) incorporating a drug molecule as a competing permeant species with low conductance. The model will be integrated with a well-established sequential two-step priming scheme for synaptic vesicles. Using pre-2026 computational tools (NEURON or custom differential equation solvers in Python/Julia), we will simulate the competition between Ca²⁺ and the blocker, calculate the resultant nanodomain [Ca²⁺], and predict its impact on priming state transitions and release probability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biophysically grounded link between classical channel pharmacology and presynaptic release models. It will yield testable predictions for how specific blockers alter paired-pulse ratios and synaptic strength, directly connecting molecular drug action to the physiological concepts of vesicle priming and presynaptic plasticity studied in Dr. Neher's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While channel block is well-studied, modeling the blocker explicitly as a permeant ion to quantify its impact on presynaptic calcium microdomains and subsequent vesicle priming states is a novel synthesis of ion channel biophysics and presynaptic physiology.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating ClC-1 Chloride Channel Function: A Quantitative Analysis of Its Role in Neuronal and Muscle Excitability Under Physiological and Pathological Conditions
proposals.motivation: The skeletal muscle chloride channel ClC-1 is classically described as purely inhibitory, stabilizing the resting potential and preventing hyperexcitability. However, pre-2026 work on other ClC family members (e.g., ClC-2) and neuronal chloride transporters shows that chloride's driving force and functional impact are highly context-dependent. This proposal challenges the canonical view by systematically testing whether specific conditions could invert ClC-1's effect, which would fundamentally alter our understanding of excitability regulation in health and disease.
proposals.hypothesis: Under specific conditions of altered intracellular chloride concentration ([Cl-]i) or membrane potential, the outwardly rectifying current through ClC-1 can depolarize the membrane potential and lower the action potential threshold, thereby enhancing cellular excitability.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined electrophysiological and computational approach. First, we will use patch-clamp recordings from HEK293 cells expressing ClC-1 and from mouse skeletal muscle fibers, manipulating [Cl-]i via pipette solutions and activity. Second, we will integrate precise ClC-1 biophysical models (based on pre-2026 structures and kinetics) into detailed compartmental models of motor neuron terminals and muscle fibers. The simulations will systematically vary parameters (e.g., resting potential, [Cl-]i, channel density) to identify conditions where the ClC-1 Nernst potential is positive to threshold. Finally, we will test these predictions in disease models (e.g., myotonia) where ClC-1 function is compromised.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative test of the ClC-1 dogma, potentially revealing a previously unrecognized pro-excitatory role. It will establish a novel framework for understanding how ion channel function is conditional on the electrochemical environment, with implications for interpreting channelopathies and designing targeted therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While the inhibitory role of ClC-1 is textbook knowledge, the deliberate search for conditions where its biophysical properties could produce paradoxical excitation is novel. This shifts the question from 'what it does' to 'under what precise conditions its effect inverts,' applying a principle known for synaptic inhibition to a major postsynaptic conductance.

proposals.title: Uniformity of ClC-1 Chloride Channel Density and Regulation in Fast-Twitch and Slow-Twitch Muscle Fibers
proposals.motivation: The ClC-1 chloride channel is critical for stabilizing the muscle fiber resting potential and controlling excitability. Pre-2026 work has established its role in myotonia, but it is unknown whether its functional expression is tuned to the distinct firing patterns and calcium dynamics of different fiber types. Determining if ClC-1 properties are fiber-type-specific is essential for understanding differential susceptibility to channelopathies and for modeling neuromuscular transmission precisely.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ClC-1 channel density, unitary conductance, and voltage-dependent gating are uniform across fast-twitch (type II) and slow-twitch (type I) muscle fibers, implying a conserved mechanism for membrane stabilization independent of contractile specialization.
proposals.method: We will use a sequential two-step approach combining electrophysiology and quantitative immunofluorescence. First, chloride conductance (gCl) will be measured in identified fiber types from mouse soleus (slow) and EDL (fast) muscles using a two-intracellular-microelectrode technique under voltage clamp with specific ClC-1 blockers (9-anthracene carboxylic acid). Second, we will employ super-resolution STORM microscopy on fixed tissue sections using validated anti-ClC-1 antibodies to quantify channel density and subsarcolemmal distribution. Data will be analyzed with custom algorithms for single-molecule localization and cluster analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative comparison of ClC-1 function between major muscle fiber types. It will establish a foundational biophysical parameter for computational models of muscle excitability and refine our understanding of how synaptic inputs are integrated at the postsynaptic membrane across different motor units.
proposals.novelty: While synaptic vesicle priming and calcium buffering have been studied across neuron types, an analogous systematic investigation of a key postsynaptic ion channel's uniformity across muscle fiber types—a fundamental cellular specialization—has not been performed. Applying Neher's quantitative, step-wise priming analysis framework to this structural-functional question is novel.

proposals.title: A Kinetic Model of ClC-1 Gating Dynamics: Bridging Synaptic Priming Concepts to Ion Channel Regulation
proposals.motivation: The skeletal muscle chloride channel ClC-1 is crucial for stabilizing membrane potential, and its dysfunction causes myotonia congenita. Pre-2026, its complex voltage-dependent gating is modulated by intracellular signals like pH and ATP, but a quantitative, dynamic model linking these regulatory inputs to specific gating transitions is lacking. Such a model is essential to predict how pathological mutations or physiological modulation alter channel open probability and muscle excitability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the multi-step gating kinetics of ClC-1 can be accurately described by a sequential, multi-state kinetic scheme analogous to synaptic vesicle priming, where regulatory signals (e.g., pH, [ATP]) act as allosteric modulators of specific transition rates, thereby setting the channel's operational mode.
proposals.method: We will adapt the sequential, multi-state kinetic modeling framework used for synaptic vesicle priming and fusion (e.g., from Neher's recent work) to ClC-1 gating. Using pre-2026 single-channel and macroscopic electrophysiological data, we will construct a Markov state model in NEURON or MATLAB. The model will include distinct closed, open, and inactivated states, with rate constants for transitions modulated by voltage and ligand binding. We will employ maximum likelihood fitting and Monte Carlo optimization algorithms to constrain the model parameters and test its predictive power against existing datasets of ClC-1 behavior under varying pH and ATP.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive computational model of ClC-1 gating that integrates biophysical and biochemical regulation, offering a quantitative tool to predict the functional impact of disease-causing mutations. It will establish a novel conceptual bridge between the well-developed kinetic theories of synaptic transmission and the modulation of ion channels, potentially revealing common principles of molecular switching.
proposals.novelty: Applying the sophisticated, sequential priming models from presynaptic physiology to the distinct problem of ClC-1 chloride channel gating is a novel cross-disciplinary approach. It moves beyond standard Hodgkin-Huxley-style models to a more mechanistic, state-based description that can explicitly incorporate allosteric modulation.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Dynamics of ClC-1 Chloride Channels Reveal a Two-Step Gating Mechanism Modulated by Synaptic Activity
proposals.motivation: The ClC-1 chloride channel is critical for stabilizing the muscle membrane potential, and its dysfunction causes myotonia. While its macroscopic biophysics are well-characterized, the real-time, single-molecule conformational changes underlying its unique double-barreled, voltage-dependent gating remain opaque. Observing these dynamics directly is essential to understand how synaptic activity and intracellular signaling might fine-tune membrane stability via ClC-1 regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the prototypical double-gate of ClC-1 operates via a sequential, two-step conformational change at the single-molecule level, and that this gating kinetics are directly modulated by presynaptic-like changes in intracellular ionic milieu, linking channel dynamics to synaptic strength paradigms.
proposals.method: We will employ single-molecule Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (smFRET) on purified, fluorescently labeled ClC-1 channels reconstituted into lipid nanodiscs. A custom microfluidic system will allow rapid exchange of solutions mimicking presynaptic terminal conditions (e.g., varying [Cl-], [Ca2+], pH). Single-channel patch-clamp recordings will be performed in parallel on the same preparations to correlate smFRET states with conductance. Hidden Markov modeling will be used to identify and quantify the kinetics of discrete conformational steps from smFRET trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct visualization of the conformational landscape of a double-barreled ClC channel, defining the elementary steps of its gating cycle. It will establish a foundational single-molecule framework for understanding how ClC-1, and by analogy neuronal ClC homologs, could be regulated by activity-dependent factors, potentially bridging muscle and synaptic excitability control.
proposals.novelty: Applying single-molecule biophysical techniques, refined for synaptic vesicle priming studies, to the structurally distinct ClC-1 channel to dissect its unique gating mechanism is a novel synthesis. The explicit investigation of how presynaptic-like ionic shifts modulate these single-channel dynamics directly connects Neher's expertise in synaptic physiology to fundamental channel biophysics.

proposals.title: Information-Theoretic Analysis of Spontaneous Vesicle Fusion as a Carrier of Presynaptic State Information
proposals.motivation: Synaptic neuroscience has traditionally treated spontaneous miniature release events as background noise, filtering them to study evoked, signal-locked transmission. However, the stochastic nature of vesicle priming and fusion is governed by the molecular machinery whose state (e.g., priming factors, calcium buffers, vesicle pool dynamics) determines synaptic strength and plasticity. This 'noise' may therefore encode rich information about the presynaptic terminal's functional state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal and amplitude statistics of spontaneous synaptic vesicle fusion events constitute an information channel that reports on real-time changes in presynaptic parameters, such as the size of the readily releasable pool, the priming rate, and the calcium sensitivity of the release machinery.
proposals.method: We will perform long-duration, high-resolution patch-clamp recordings (postsynaptic) and capacitance measurements (presynaptic) at cultured neocortical synapses under controlled intracellular calcium buffering. The recorded traces of spontaneous activity will be analyzed using a custom computational pipeline. This pipeline will employ a hidden Markov model (HMM) to segment activity into distinct kinetic states, coupled with a sequential Bayesian inference algorithm to estimate underlying presynaptic parameters (e.g., pool size N, release probability p) from the observed pattern of miniature events. This 'inverse problem' approach inverts the standard use of such models to simulate output.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for non-invasively assaying presynaptic function through spontaneous release analysis. It could reveal how short-term plasticity phenomena like facilitation and depression are reflected in ongoing 'background' activity, providing a continuous readout of synaptic state without evoked stimulation.
proposals.novelty: The paradigm shift lies in treating the signal as noise and the noise as signal. While spontaneous release is well-characterized physiologically, its systematic exploitation as an information channel to infer dynamic presynaptic parameters in real time is a novel, non-obvious application of information theory to subcellular biophysics.

proposals.title: Active Dendritic Filtering and Synaptic Information Transfer: A Computational Study Linking Nonlinear Conductances to Vesicle Release Probability
proposals.motivation: Classical cable theory describes passive signal attenuation in dendrites, but real neurons possess strongly active, nonlinear voltage-gated conductances that can amplify or filter synaptic inputs. How these active properties influence the fidelity of presynaptic action potential propagation into terminals and, consequently, the probabilistic release of synaptic vesicles remains poorly quantified. This gap limits our understanding of how intrinsic membrane dynamics contribute to synaptic diversity and short-term plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strong, spatially heterogeneous active conductances in presynaptic terminals and axons act as a nonlinear filter, introducing history-dependent information loss that systematically modulates the calcium influx driving vesicle priming and fusion. This filtering mechanism contributes to the diversity of synaptic strength observed in sequential priming models.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-compartment cable model of a neocortical axon and bouton, incorporating Hodgkin-Huxley-type sodium/potassium channels and high-threshold calcium channels (e.g., N- or P/Q-type) based on pre-2026 biophysical data. Using a stochastic vesicle release model (e.g., a calcium-domain model coupled to a two-step priming scheme), we will simulate trains of action potentials. Information loss will be quantified via mutual information between input spike timing/rate and the resulting calcium transients and release events. Simulations will be performed using the NEURON simulation environment, with parameter sweeps on channel density and localization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework linking active membrane biophysics directly to presynaptic short-term plasticity and the heterogeneity of release probability. It will predict how specific conductance distributions can create 'release hotspots' or filters, offering a biophysical explanation for the spectrum of fully-primed to slowly-recovering vesicle pools observed in Neher's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While active dendrites are studied postsynaptically, applying this concept presynaptically to explain information loss and vesicle release statistics is novel. It integrates Neher's expertise in vesicle priming with the biophysics of active membranes, moving beyond purely calcium-centric views of release modulation.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Experimental Bottlenecks on the Measured Maximal Rate of Synaptic Vesicle Fusion
proposals.motivation: The maximal rate constant of vesicle fusion is a fundamental parameter in models of synaptic transmission and short-term plasticity, such as the sequential two-step priming scheme. However, pre-2026 experiments using flash photolysis of caged Ca2+ to measure this rate may be confounded by technical limitations, including the finite speed of Ca2+ uncaging and the depletion of a readily releasable pool of vesicles. This obscures the true kinetic ceiling of the release machinery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the experimentally measured maximal fusion rate constant is not an intrinsic property of the release machinery but is instead constrained by the speed of Ca2+ elevation and the availability of vesicles in a fully-primed, fusion-competent state.
proposals.method: We will combine high-resolution presynaptic Ca2+ imaging with ultrafast, spatially uniform flash photolysis using a digital micromirror device to achieve sub-millisecond Ca2+ steps. At the calyx of Held, we will measure fusion rates via postsynaptic recordings while pharmacologically and genetically manipulating the size of the readily releasable pool. A kinetic model incorporating vesicle priming states and Ca2+ sensor dynamics will be fitted to the data to deconvolve experimental artifacts from intrinsic fusion kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a corrected, intrinsic maximal fusion rate, providing a more accurate parameter for quantitative models of synaptic transmission. It will clarify whether diversity in synaptic strength arises from differences in intrinsic fusion kinetics or from experimental constraints, directly testing predictions of recent priming models.
proposals.novelty: While limitations of photolysis are known, systematically quantifying their impact on the measured maximal rate—and separating it from vesicle availability constraints—within the framework of modern multi-step priming models is novel. This moves beyond simply measuring the rate to defining its true biophysical determinants.

proposals.title: Synaptotagmin-1 cooperativity and the molecular basis of the high-affinity four-calcium sensor for fast exocytosis
proposals.motivation: The precise molecular identity and stoichiometry of the calcium sensor that triggers synaptic vesicle fusion remains a central, unresolved question in presynaptic physiology. While synaptotagmin-1 is the leading candidate, its two C2 domains can bind only two or three calcium ions, leaving the requirement for four ions, inferred from the steep Ca2+ dependence of release, mechanistically unexplained. Determining the complete sensor is essential for understanding the fundamental trigger of neurotransmission and its modulation in short-term plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high-affinity, four-calcium sensor is a cooperative complex formed by synaptotagmin-1 and an auxiliary protein (potentially complexin or a SNARE subunit), where the auxiliary component contributes an additional calcium-binding site, enabling the observed high Ca2+ cooperativity and rapid triggering of fusion.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined approach of in vitro reconstitution and high-resolution electrophysiology. First, we will reconstitute fusion using proteoliposomes containing neuronal SNAREs, synaptotagmin-1, and candidate auxiliary proteins. Fusion kinetics will be measured via lipid-mixing assays under rapid Ca2+ uncaging. Second, we will perform high-resolution capacitance measurements and flash photolysis of caged Ca2+ in calyx of Held terminals, comparing release kinetics following genetic perturbation (e.g., knockdown or point mutations) of candidate auxiliary proteins. The data will be analyzed using kinetic modeling based on sequential priming schemes to identify the molecular step conferring the fourth Ca2+ binding site.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive molecular model for the Ca2+ sensor of fast exocytosis, resolving the long-standing discrepancy between the biophysical cooperativity and known protein biochemistry. It will establish whether synaptotagmin-1 acts alone or in a complex, fundamentally advancing our mechanistic understanding of the synaptic trigger and providing a framework for studying modulation in diverse synaptic states.
proposals.novelty: The proposal moves beyond the established role of synaptotagmin-1 to test the non-obvious hypothesis that the four-ion sensor is a heteromeric complex, directly integrating the concepts of vesicle priming states and molecular cooperativity to explain the Ca2+ dependence of release.

proposals.title: Direct Measurement of Calcium-Dependent Vesicle Priming and Fusion Kinetics in Mammalian Central Synapses Using Flash Photolysis
proposals.motivation: Kinetic models of synaptic vesicle priming and fusion, such as the sequential two-step priming scheme, have been developed and tested primarily in model systems like the calyx of Held. It remains unknown whether the kinetic parameters and the fundamental relationship between calcium, priming, and the size of the readily releasable pool (RRP) are conserved in the diverse, smaller terminals of the mammalian central nervous system (CNS). Validating these models in native CNS circuits is crucial for understanding the presynaptic basis of synaptic strength, short-term plasticity, and computation in the brain.
proposals.hypothesis: The kinetic parameters governing calcium-dependent vesicle priming and fusion, derived from the calyx of Held, will differ significantly when measured directly in mammalian cortical or hippocampal synapses, reflecting adaptations for diverse computational demands.
proposals.method: We will employ flash photolysis of caged calcium (e.g., DM-nitrophen) in acute brain slices from rodents, targeting presynaptic terminals in layer 2/3 cortical or CA1 hippocampal synapses. Whole-cell recordings from postsynaptic neurons will measure EPSCs evoked by brief, spatially restricted calcium uncaging pulses. By varying the timing and amplitude of the calcium step, we will apply established kinetic analysis (Neher & Sakaba, 2008) to derive the calcium dependence of priming and fusion rates, the size of the RRP, and the recovery from depression, directly comparing these parameters to those from the calyx.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative comparison of presynaptic calcium kinetics between a canonical model synapse and native mammalian CNS terminals. It will test the universality of current priming models and reveal how kinetic tuning contributes to synapse-specific functional diversity. The findings will bridge a critical gap between biophysical models and cortical circuit physiology.
proposals.novelty: While flash photolysis has been used at the calyx of Held, its application to measure precise priming and fusion kinetics in small, embedded CNS synapses is technically challenging and has not been systematically achieved, making a direct parameter comparison novel.

proposals.title: Quantifying Guard Cell Ion Channel Densities and Gating Kinetics via Non-Stationary Single-Channel Noise Analysis
proposals.motivation: Guard cell turgor, which controls stomatal apertures for gas exchange and water conservation, is governed by ion fluxes through potassium and anion channels. However, precise estimates of functional channel densities and their kinetic states in the intact guard cell plasma membrane are lacking, limiting quantitative models of stomatal control. Techniques from neuroscience for analyzing channel 'mover' dynamics in small compartments could bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the macroscopic current fluctuations in guard cells are dominated by a limited number of ion channels, and that spectral analysis of these fluctuations can reveal distinct channel densities and priming-like kinetic states analogous to synaptic vesicle pools.
proposals.method: We will apply non-stationary fluctuation analysis (NSFA) and power spectral density (PSD) fitting, techniques refined for neuronal synapses and patches, to whole-cell recordings from *Arabidopsis thaliana* guard cells. Current variance will be plotted against mean current during voltage steps to derive single-channel conductance and number of functional channels. PSDs of steady-state noise will be fitted with Lorentzian functions to extract characteristic corner frequencies, reporting on channel gating kinetics. This 'step-term' analysis will be applied under varying cytosolic calcium buffering conditions to probe kinetic state transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, non-invasive measurements of functional ion channel density and dominant gating modes in guard cells. It will establish a quantitative framework linking channel population dynamics to macroscopic stomatal responses, analogous to the quantal analysis of synaptic transmission, thereby enhancing predictive models of plant water use and carbon uptake.
proposals.novelty: Applying advanced neuronal noise analysis to plant electrophysiology is novel, and the conceptual translation of 'priming' and 'recovery' kinetic states from synaptic vesicles to ion channel populations in guard cells represents a significant cross-disciplinary innovation.

proposals.title: Dissociating Molecular Priming from Ca2+ Channel Coupling: A Sequential Two-Step Pathway for Vesicle Release
proposals.motivation: The molecular mechanisms that determine synaptic strength and short-term plasticity remain incompletely defined. While priming (making vesicles release-ready) and the physical coupling of vesicles to voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCCs) are both critical for release probability, it is unclear if they are mechanistically linked or represent distinct, separable processes. Understanding this relationship is key to explaining the diversity of synaptic properties observed in neural circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that molecular priming and tight spatial coupling to VGCCs are distinct, sequential stages in the vesicle release pathway, where priming is necessary but not sufficient for establishing nanodomain coupling.
proposals.method: We will combine high-resolution presynaptic Ca2+ uncaging with paired-pulse protocols and pharmacological manipulation of priming factors (e.g., Munc13, CAPS) in rodent neocortical synapses. Using a stepwise experimental paradigm, we will first assess the vesicle's molecular priming state via its recovery from depression. Subsequently, we will probe the coupling efficiency by analyzing the Ca2+ sensitivity and kinetics of release evoked by uncaging, independent of VGCC activation. This will allow us to isolate the contribution of each stage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct experimental test of the sequential two-step priming model, moving beyond correlation to establish causality. It will define whether coupling is a downstream consequence of priming or an independently regulated process, offering a new framework for understanding how specific molecular machineries tune synaptic efficacy and short-term plasticity.
proposals.novelty: While priming and coupling are known to correlate, directly testing their causal sequence and separability by manipulating one while measuring the other in a single synapse is a novel approach. This moves past descriptive models to a mechanistic dissection of the release pathway.

proposals.title: Dynamic Stability of Vesicle-Channel Nanodomains During Sustained High-Frequency Activity and Synaptic Depression
proposals.motivation: Short-term synaptic depression (STD) is a fundamental computational feature of neural circuits, often attributed to vesicle depletion and release site inactivation. However, the stability of the physical coupling between voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCCs) and primed vesicles within the active zone nanodomain during sustained activity remains poorly characterized. Understanding whether this coupling is static or dynamically modulated during high-frequency firing is crucial for mechanistic models of synaptic plasticity and information processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-frequency firing and the resultant local calcium dynamics induce a progressive, activity-dependent destabilization of the vesicle-VGCC nanodomain coupling, which constitutes a novel and significant contributor to synaptic depression beyond vesicle depletion.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution patch-clamp electrophysiology and presynaptic calcium uncaging at the calyx of Held, combined with variance-mean analysis of EPSCs to isolate release probability (P<sub>r</sub>) changes. To directly probe coupling stability, we will use a two-step priming assay: a strong, rapid calcium uncaging pulse will trigger release from the fully-primed, tightly coupled vesicle pool, followed by a test depolarization to assess the remaining functional coupling of vesicles that failed to release. By repeating this paired-pulse protocol during sustained 10-100 Hz train stimulation, we can dissect the contribution of coupling stability decay to STD, distinct from the depletion of the readily releasable pool.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative framework for how nanodomain organization withstands physiological activity, directly testing the 'sequential two-step priming' model under dynamic conditions. It will identify whether coupling destabilization is a core mechanism of STD, refining canonical depletion-based models and linking short-term plasticity to the molecular dynamics of the release machinery.
proposals.novelty: While vesicle pools and calcium sensitivity are well-studied, directly assessing the real-time stability of the physical vesicle-channel coupling during sustained activity is a novel approach. This moves beyond correlative measures of release probability to a causal manipulation of the coupling state itself.

proposals.title: A Sequential Priming Framework for Inhibitory Synapses: Quantifying Chloride Dynamics and Vesicle Pools in GABAergic Short-Term Plasticity
proposals.motivation: While the quantitative frameworks for presynaptic vesicle dynamics and calcium-dependent priming are well-established for excitatory synapses, a parallel mechanistic understanding of inhibitory transmission is lacking. Inhibitory synaptic strength and plasticity are critically governed by postsynaptic chloride dynamics, which determine the driving force for GABA_A receptor currents, but how presynaptic vesicle pool dynamics interact with these variable chloride gradients remains unclear. Applying the rigorous, quantifiable priming models developed for excitatory synapses to inhibitory transmission would bridge a significant gap in synaptic physiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diversity in inhibitory synaptic strength and short-term plasticity can be reproduced by a sequential two-step priming scheme for GABAergic vesicles, where the efficacy of transmission is modulated by the dynamic interaction between presynaptic release probability and activity-dependent shifts in postsynaptic chloride concentration.
proposals.method: We will combine pre-2026 established techniques: dual whole-cell recordings from connected pairs of hippocampal or neocortical neurons, with the presynaptic pipette containing a chloride-sensitive dye (e.g., MQAE) to monitor presynaptic [Cl-]_i. We will use established presynaptic capacitance measurements and paired-pulse protocols to assay vesicle pool dynamics and priming states. The data will be fit to a modified version of the sequential priming model, where the postsynaptic response is a function of both the quantal release event and a dynamically calculated chloride driving force based on measured [Cl-]_i and the activity of cation-chloride cotransporters (e.g., KCC2).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified model that integrates presynaptic vesicle state dynamics with postsynaptic chloride homeostasis to predict inhibitory synaptic efficacy. It will establish whether inhibitory synapses utilize distinct priming kinetics or pool sizes compared to excitatory synapses. The findings will offer a new computational framework for understanding how perturbations in chloride regulation, as seen in neuropathologies, fundamentally alter network inhibition.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly applying the quantitative, state-based modeling of presynaptic vesicle pools—a paradigm developed for calcium-triggered exocytosis—to the distinct biophysical context of inhibitory transmission, where chloride is the key variable rather than calcium. This shifts the focus from the trigger of release to the modulation of its electrochemical consequence.

proposals.title: Quantitative Dissection of Release Kinetics: A Common Priming-Fusion Framework for Neuronal and Non-Neuronal Exocytosis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work has established that synaptic vesicle release is governed by a precisely orchestrated, calcium-dependent priming and fusion machinery, with diversity arising from distinct vesicle pools and priming states. In contrast, release from non-neuronal cells (e.g., endocrine cells) is often described as slower and mechanistically distinct. This perceived qualitative difference may obscure a unified exocytotic principle, where kinetics are primarily shaped by local calcium dynamics rather than fundamentally different molecular hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the kinetic differences between fast neuronal and slower non-neuronal exocytosis are not qualitative but arise from quantitative differences in the calcium microdomain experienced by the vesicle release machinery, which can be explained within a common sequential priming framework.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution patch-clamp capacitance measurements and flash photolysis of caged Ca2+ in adrenal chromaffin cells (non-neuronal model) and calyx of Held synapses (neuronal model). Using pre-2026 established kinetic models of sequential priming (e.g., Neher's two-step scheme), we will fit the release time courses evoked by precisely defined [Ca2+]i steps. The core analysis will involve comparing the rate constants for priming and fusion steps between cell types under matched bulk [Ca2+]i, and then using 3D reaction-diffusion modeling (e.g., MCell) to simulate how differing spatial geometries of calcium channels and vesicles create distinct effective calcium microdomains that quantitatively alter these rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unifying quantitative model that reconciles disparate exocytotic phenomena across cell types under a single mechanistic umbrella. It will directly test whether the conceptual framework of vesicle priming states, central to synaptic physiology, is universally applicable, thereby bridging neurobiology and cell biology. The findings will refine our understanding of how cellular architecture tailors a core release machinery to specific physiological functions.
proposals.novelty: While differences in release speed are well-known, proposing that the established neuronal priming/fusion sequence fully accounts for non-neuronal release—with kinetics set solely by calcium microdomain geometry—is a novel and parsimonious synthesis. It challenges the prevailing view of fundamentally distinct release modes.

proposals.title: A Comparative Analysis of Vesicle Fusion Delays: Testing the Universality of the Priming-Fusion Sequence Across Diverse Secretory Cell Types
proposals.motivation: In neurons, the delay between Ca2+ influx and vesicle fusion is a critical determinant of synaptic transmission speed and plasticity, governed by molecular priming steps. It is unknown whether this mechanistic sequence—where vesicles must be fully primed before a final, rapid fusion step—is a specialized feature of fast synapses or a universal principle of regulated exocytosis. Determining if a similar fusion delay exists in endocrine cells, neuroendocrine cells, and immune cells would reveal fundamental constraints on the secretory machinery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a measurable, Ca2+-dependent fusion delay is a conserved feature across professional secretory cell types, but its duration and molecular determinants vary systematically with the physiological demands of the secretory pathway.
proposals.method: We will apply high-resolution, pre-2026 electrochemical methods (amperometry with carbon-fiber microelectrodes) and capacitance measurements to directly quantify the delay between a precisely timed Ca2+ uncaging pulse (using UV flash photolysis of NP-EGTA) and the onset of single-vesicle fusion events. This will be performed in acutely isolated or primary cultured cells: pancreatic beta cells (insulin), chromaffin cells (catecholamines), mast cells (histamine), and, for comparison, calyx of Held nerve terminals (glutamate). Data will be analyzed using established algorithms for event detection (e.g., Thurley et al., 2014) and kinetic fitting to distinguish priming and fusion-pore expansion steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a comparative map of fusion kinetics across cell biology, directly testing the universality of the priming-fusion sequence. It will link cell-specific molecular toolkits (e.g., synaptotagmin isoforms, Munc13 variants, complexin) to functional output kinetics, providing a physiological framework for understanding secretory diversity in health and disease.
proposals.novelty: While fusion delays are studied in neurons, a systematic cross-cell-type comparison using the same high-temporal-resolution methodology has not been performed. This moves beyond cataloging differences in release probability to probe the conserved core timing mechanism of the vesicle cycle itself.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Determinants of Synaptic Delay: A Systematic Analysis of Calcium Influx Dynamics on Vesicle Fusion Timing
proposals.motivation: The precise timing of synaptic vesicle fusion is critical for information processing in neural circuits, yet the quantitative relationship between presynaptic calcium dynamics and the delay to release remains poorly defined. While the role of calcium in triggering release is well-established, pre-2026 work from Neher's group and others highlights a complex priming landscape and diverse vesicle pools, suggesting that the kinetics of calcium influx may selectively influence distinct release pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the delay to vesicle fusion is not a fixed property but is dynamically modulated by the amplitude and duration of presynaptic depolarization, with distinct calcium sensor domains or vesicle priming states exhibiting differential sensitivity to these temporal parameters.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution presynaptic patch-clamp recordings from cultured hippocampal neurons, paired with capacitance measurements to monitor exocytosis. Using a voltage-clamp protocol, we will systematically vary the duration (0.5-50 ms) and amplitude of depolarizing steps to manipulate calcium influx. This will be combined with ultrafast local calcium uncaging at the active zone to separate voltage-dependent from purely calcium-dependent effects. Data will be analyzed by fitting release kinetics with a sequential two-step priming model, explicitly testing how model parameters (e.g., priming rates, fusion probability) depend on the specific calcium transient profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, predictive framework linking presynaptic action potential shape to synaptic delay and release probability. It will directly test predictions of existing priming models regarding kinetic diversity and could reveal how short-term plasticity is influenced by the history of calcium dynamics at the terminal.
proposals.novelty: While calcium's role in release is known, systematically dissecting its effect on fusion *timing* by independently varying influx duration and amplitude, and integrating this with a explicit priming model, represents a novel approach to understanding the microsecond-scale precision of synaptic transmission.

proposals.title: Simultaneous Capacitance and Electrochemical Measurements Resolve the Kinetics of Vesicle Priming, Fusion, and Membrane Retrieval at Single Synapses
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in presynaptic physiology is to directly correlate the quantal release of neurotransmitter, measured electrochemically, with the precise membrane area changes associated with vesicle fusion and retrieval, measured via capacitance. While both techniques exist, their combined application at small central synapses has been limited, leaving a gap in our understanding of how molecular priming steps (e.g., from recent work by Neher) translate into real-time membrane dynamics and release probability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that concurrent high-resolution capacitance and amperometric recordings will reveal distinct kinetic signatures for the fusion of vesicles from different priming states (e.g., fully-primed vs. super-primed), and that the rate of membrane retrieval is directly coupled to the preceding fusion event's characteristics.
proposals.method: We will employ dual recordings in cultured hippocampal or neocortical neurons. Whole-cell patch-clamp will be used for capacitance measurements (Cm) using a phase-tracking algorithm (Lindau-Neher technique) with high temporal resolution (~1 ms). Concurrently, a carbon-fiber microelectrode placed adjacent to a presynaptic bouton will detect quantal catecholamine (or serotonin) release via amperometry. Presynaptic Ca2+ dynamics will be controlled via uncaging or voltage-clamp steps. A custom-written analysis routine will temporally align amperometric spike events (fusion pores) with step changes in Cm (fusion) and subsequent exponential decays (retrieval).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, temporally precise correlation between quantal release events and membrane area changes at central nervous system synapses. It will test and refine existing sequential priming models by assigning specific kinetic parameters to the fusion of vesicles from different functional pools. The data will constrain models of short-term plasticity by linking vesicle pool dynamics to measurable membrane capacitance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous application of these two gold-standard biophysical techniques at small central synapses, moving beyond correlative studies to establish direct cause-and-effect relationships between membrane trafficking events and neurotransmitter release in real time.

proposals.title: A Quantitative Boundary for Step-like Calcium Approximations: Defining the Calcium-Action Potential Waveform Divergence Index (C-APWDI)
proposals.motivation: Synaptic release probability and short-term plasticity are fundamentally governed by the spatiotemporal profile of presynaptic calcium, which is typically approximated as a uniform, step-like rise in theoretical models. However, the validity of this simplification across diverse action potential (AP) waveforms and firing patterns remains untested, potentially introducing significant error in models of vesicle priming and fusion dynamics. This gap limits our ability to accurately link presynaptic calcium dynamics to the sequential priming schemes and synaptic diversity central to Neher's recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantifiable 'Calcium-AP Waveform Divergence Index' (C-APWDI) can be predicted from AP shape and firing pattern, and that this index defines a clear boundary beyond which step-like calcium approximations generate misleading predictions for calcium-dependent synaptic processes.
proposals.method: We will perform simultaneous whole-cell somatic and dendritic patch-clamp recordings of AP waveforms and high-speed GCaMP8f imaging in rodent cortical and hippocampal slices. Data will be collected across neuron types (e.g., pyramidal vs. interneuron) and firing patterns (single spikes, bursts, theta-frequency trains). The C-APWDI will be calculated as a normalized integral of the difference between the measured GCaMP transient and an idealized step function fitted to its amplitude. A biophysical model, incorporating known calcium buffer dynamics (e.g., EGTA, BAPTA kinetics) and diffusion, will be built to predict the C-APWDI from input parameters (AP half-width, afterhyperpolarization depth, burst frequency, dendritic distance).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for assessing the error introduced by the common step-like calcium approximation, directly impacting the interpretation of presynaptic plasticity experiments. It will yield a predictive tool for experimentalists to determine when detailed AP waveform recording is necessary for accurate modeling of vesicle release dynamics, thereby refining models of synaptic strength and short-term plasticity.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of step-like approximations are conceptually acknowledged, this study moves beyond qualitative critique by developing a measurable, predictive index. It uniquely integrates high-fidelity electrophysiology, calcium imaging, and biophysical modeling to explicitly map the parameter space where the simplification fails.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Calcium Domain: How Spatially and Temporally Complex Action Potential Signals Modulate Vesicle Priming and Fusion Kinetics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 models of synaptic transmission often use uniform, step-like calcium elevations as a proxy for the action potential-evoked signal. However, physiological calcium influx through voltage-gated channels is highly localized and transient, creating complex nanodomains. This simplification may obscure key mechanisms underlying the diversity in synaptic strength and short-term plasticity documented in recent priming studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatially and temporally complex calcium signal following an action potential, not a uniform step, is the critical determinant for the sequential two-step priming of vesicles and the differential recruitment of slowly-recovering, fully-primed vesicle pools.
proposals.method: We will combine pre-2026 established techniques: (1) High-resolution calcium uncaging with 2-photon glutamate imaging to map the spatiotemporal calcium profile during simulated action potential waveforms. (2) Pair this with capacitance measurements and deconvolution of EPSCs to quantify priming and fusion kinetics with sub-millisecond resolution. (3) Develop a computational model using a modified version of the 'sequential two-step priming' framework, where calcium-dependent rate constants are driven by simulated nanodomain dynamics rather than bulk concentration.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the physiological validity of a core modeling assumption, linking specific features of the action potential waveform to distinct vesicle pool dynamics. It will provide a revised, biophysically grounded framework to explain diversity in synaptic strength and plasticity based on presynaptic calcium microarchitecture.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of calcium domains is known, this proposal uniquely challenges the step-rise paradigm in the specific context of modern, multi-step priming models and tests its impact on the kinetics of defined, physiologically relevant vesicle pools like those mediating presynaptic LTP.

proposals.title: Quantitative mapping of nanodomain calcium transients at active zones during physiological action potentials
proposals.motivation: The amplitude and kinetics of calcium influx at release sites are the primary determinants of synaptic vesicle release probability and short-term plasticity. However, existing measurements of action potential-evoked calcium transients are often from bulk compartments or rely on exogenous buffers, failing to capture the true, local signal sensed by the release machinery. This gap limits our quantitative understanding of how presynaptic calcium dynamics govern the diversity of synaptic strength and plasticity described in recent priming models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local calcium transient at a release site during a single action potential is smaller in amplitude and faster in decay kinetics than previously estimated from averaged or buffered measurements, and that its properties are precisely tuned to the priming state of the docked vesicle.
proposals.method: We will perform dual-recordings from connected neocortical neurons in acute brain slices. The presynaptic terminal will be loaded with a low-affinity, fast calcium indicator (e.g., Fluo-5F or OGB-5N) via the patch pipette, combined with a morphological dye. Using high-speed, spinning-disk or two-photon laser-scanning microscopy with optimized detection, we will record calcium transients evoked by single action potentials at individual boutons. We will apply established noise-subtraction and deconvolution algorithms to estimate the underlying calcium signal. This will be correlated with postsynaptic responses and manipulated using established priming protocols (e.g., paired-pulse facilitation, slow recovery from depression) to link calcium dynamics to functional vesicle pools.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative measurements of the physiological calcium nanodomain signal that triggers vesicle fusion in central synapses. It will establish a foundational biophysical parameter set for modeling synaptic transmission and allow a direct test of how the 'sequential two-step priming' scheme is governed by local calcium dynamics, bridging molecular priming mechanisms and functional output.
proposals.novelty: While calcium imaging in terminals is established, this proposal focuses on capturing the unperturbed, local transient during a single physiological action potential by combining low-affinity indicators, high-temporal-resolution imaging, and correlation with functional release states, moving beyond averaged or pharmacologically isolated signals.

proposals.title: Direct Biophysical Characterization of the Synaptotagmin-1 Calcium Sensor: Resolving Intrinsic Binding Kinetics and Fusion Rate Constants in a Defined In Vitro Fusion System
proposals.motivation: Despite the central role of calcium-triggered vesicle fusion in synaptic transmission, the precise molecular identity and intrinsic biophysical properties of the primary calcium sensor remain debated. While synaptotagmin-1 (Syt1) is the leading candidate, its exact calcium-binding stoichiometry, cooperativity, and the direct kinetic link from calcium binding to membrane merger are poorly quantified. This gap prevents a mechanistic, quantitative understanding of synaptic diversity and plasticity, which are modeled as variations in priming and release probability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intrinsic calcium-binding properties of purified Syt1, when reconstituted with SNARE proteins into a minimal fusion system, will exhibit cooperative binding with a Hill coefficient of ~4-5 and will directly determine the rate constant for lipid mixing in a calcium-dependent manner that matches the sub-millisecond kinetics of fast synaptic transmission.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential, two-step in vitro reconstitution approach. First, we will purify full-length Syt1 and core SNARE proteins (synaptobrevin, SNAP-25, syntaxin-1A). Second, we will reconstitute these components into two populations of proteoliposomes (donor vesicles containing Syt1 and synaptobrevin, acceptor vesicles containing syntaxin-1A and SNAP-25). Fusion kinetics will be monitored in real-time using a stopped-flow fluorometer, measuring lipid dequenching upon rapid mixing with calcium buffers. We will systematically vary calcium concentration (from 0.1 to 100 µM) and use non-linear regression to fit the resulting fusion time courses to a kinetic model, extracting the apparent calcium affinity (K_d), cooperativity (n_H), and the maximal fusion rate constant (k_fusion). Control experiments will use Syt1 mutants with impaired calcium binding (e.g., D-to-N mutations in the C2 domains).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative measurement of the intrinsic calcium-sensing and fusion-triggering parameters of the putative primary sensor in a minimal system. The derived rate constants will serve as essential, constrained parameters for next-generation computational models of synaptic transmission, moving beyond phenomenological descriptions to a molecularly grounded understanding of release probability, short-term plasticity, and the diversity of synaptic strength.
proposals.novelty: While Syt1's role is widely studied genetically and in cells, directly measuring its intrinsic biophysical constants in a defined fusion machinery, free from cellular buffers and endogenous protein pools, is a critical and unresolved challenge. This approach moves from correlation to causation in defining the sensor's properties.

proposals.title: Quantitative dissection of vesicle pool depletion and replenishment kinetics during and after defined calcium steps
proposals.motivation: Synaptic strength and short-term plasticity are fundamentally governed by the dynamics of the readily releasable pool (RRP). While recent work, including from our group, has established multi-step priming schemes, the precise kinetic interplay between calcium-triggered vesicle depletion and the subsequent, activity-dependent replenishment of the RRP remains poorly quantified. A mechanistic understanding of these coupled processes is essential to explain the diversity of synaptic performance observed in neural circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the kinetics of RRP depletion during a calcium step and its replenishment afterward are not independent but are co-regulated by a shared molecular machinery, and that the observed diversity in short-term plasticity arises from systematic variations in the rate constants governing this coupled system.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution presynaptic capacitance measurements and carbon-fiber amperometry in adrenal chromaffin cells, a model system with a large, homogeneous RRP. Using established whole-cell voltage-clamp protocols, we will apply precisely timed, physiological calcium steps via flash photolysis of caged Ca2+ to trigger synchronous exocytosis and deplete the RRP. The recovery of the RRP will be monitored by applying identical test steps at defined intervals. Data will be fit with kinetic models, including our sequential two-step priming scheme, using maximum-likelihood estimation and Bayesian model comparison to extract the rate constants for fusion, priming, and upstream vesicle mobilization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully quantitative, model-constrained description of the coupled depletion-replenishment cycle under defined calcium conditions. It will establish a foundational kinetic framework for predicting synaptic output during activity patterns, directly linking presynaptic biophysics to short-term plasticity phenotypes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct experimental coupling of a precisely triggered depletion phase to the measurement of replenishment kinetics, moving beyond phenomenological descriptions of plasticity to a mechanistic, parameterized model of the complete vesicle cycle driven by physiological calcium signals.

proposals.title: Signal Averaging of Synaptic Events Reveals the Calcium Dependence and Kinetics of Low-Probability, Slow Synapses
proposals.motivation: Synapses with low release probability (Pr) and slow kinetics are crucial for neural circuit computations, including sparse coding and working memory, but their biophysical properties remain poorly characterized due to high trial-to-trial variability and low signal-to-noise ratios in recordings. Traditional analysis of single trials is insufficient to resolve the precise time course and calcium sensitivity of these events, limiting our understanding of synaptic diversity and short-term plasticity mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying rigorous signal averaging techniques, analogous to those used in astronomy, to repeated stimulations of a single identified low-Pr synapse will yield a high-fidelity event template, enabling precise quantification of its kinetic properties and its sensitivity to presynaptic calcium dynamics during priming and fusion.
proposals.method: We will perform paired recordings from identified slow, low-Pr synapses (e.g., layer 5 cortical connections) and deliver hundreds of repeated, controlled action potential stimulations. Postsynaptic responses will be recorded and computationally aligned using cross-correlation algorithms. A moving-window average will construct a high-resolution template of the unitary event. This template will then be used to analyze responses under different calcium conditions (via EGTA or BAPTA buffers) and during paired-pulse protocols, directly testing predictions of sequential priming models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution functional characterization of slow, low-Pr synapses, directly testing their role in presynaptic long-term potentiation (LTP) and short-term plasticity. It will establish a robust methodological framework for analyzing highly variable synaptic transmission, bridging the gap between phenomenological models and direct physiological measurement.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of signal co-addition from observational astronomy to intracellular neurophysiology, creating a noise-resistant assay for synapses previously considered too variable for detailed kinetic analysis, thereby enabling a direct test of multi-step priming schemes at these synapses.

proposals.title: Decoupling the Priming-Fusion Sequence: A Kinetic and Molecular Analysis of Spontaneous Miniature Release Independent of Action-Potential-Driven Calcium Steps
proposals.motivation: Spontaneous miniature release is a fundamental synaptic process, but its regulation is poorly understood compared to evoked release. While evoked release is triggered by Ca2+ influx, miniature events persist in the absence of action potentials, suggesting distinct priming and fusion mechanisms. Understanding this 'inverse' process is crucial for a complete model of synaptic vesicle dynamics, as it may reveal intrinsic fusion machinery regulation and its role in synaptic homeostasis and plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spontaneous miniature release is governed by a stochastic, sequential priming scheme where vesicles in a 'fully-primed' state undergo fusion independent of rapid, global Ca2+ steps, and that this process is modulated by specific molecular 'movers' distinct from those driving evoked release.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution patch-clamp electrophysiology in cultured neocortical neurons to record miniature postsynaptic currents (mPSCs) under conditions of action potential blockade and controlled intracellular Ca2+ buffering. Using a combination of pharmacological interventions (e.g., botulinum toxins to cleave specific SNAREs) and transgenic manipulation of candidate priming proteins (e.g., Munc13, CAPS), we will dissect the molecular requirements. Kinetic analysis of mPSC intervals will be modeled using maximum-likelihood estimation to fit sequential state models, directly testing predictions from Neher's recent 'two-step priming' framework against the stochastic properties of spontaneous fusion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative kinetic and molecular description of the spontaneous release pathway, defining its relationship to the established priming steps for evoked release. It will identify specific molecular determinants that bias vesicles toward the spontaneous fusion pathway, offering new insights into how synapses maintain and adjust their functional baseline independently of activity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically applying the quantitative, step-wise priming framework—developed primarily to explain evoked release diversity—to the spontaneous release process, explicitly testing whether it represents the stochastic fusion of vesicles from the same primed pool or a parallel, molecularly distinct pathway.

proposals.title: Quantitative correction of liquid junction potentials for nano-pipettes enables precise measurement of presynaptic calcium dynamics and vesicle priming
proposals.motivation: The drive towards smaller pipettes for intracellular recording in small synaptic terminals and dendritic spines minimizes cellular disruption but amplifies errors from liquid junction potentials (LJPs). Pre-2026, standard LJP corrections (e.g., Barry/Diamond methods) assume large-tip geometries and are invalid for nano-pipettes, where tip potential and access resistance are dominant. This inaccuracy directly impedes precise measurement of absolute resting potentials and calcium influx, which are critical for studying the calcium-dependent priming and fusion steps central to Neher's recent work on synaptic diversity and LTP.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a physically explicit, finite-element model of ion diffusion and migration in a nano-cone geometry can predict and correct for the effective LJP at the pipette tip, and that applying this correction will reveal a tighter correlation between measured presynaptic resting potential and the calcium-dependent priming of synaptic vesicles.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational model using the Nernst-Planck-Poisson equations, implemented in a finite-element solver (e.g., COMSOL Multiphysics), to simulate ion concentrations and potentials in a conical nano-pipette tip filled with various intracellular solutions (e.g., K-gluconate, Cs-methanesulfonate). The model will be parameterized with empirically measured tip diameters (<100 nm) and access resistances. We will validate predictions by simultaneous whole-cell and nano-pipette recording from the same neocortical neuron, comparing corrected potentials. Finally, we will use corrected nano-pipette measurements in presynaptic terminals to correlate resting potential with vesicle pool sizes assessed using established step-depletion protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the field with a validated, open-source correction tool for nano-pipette recordings, enabling accurate intracellular potential measurements in subcellular compartments. It will directly test the influence of presynaptic resting potential on the sequential two-step priming scheme, offering a biophysical link between membrane potential, calcium driving force, and the diversity of synaptic strength.
proposals.novelty: While LJP theory is mature, its application to the non-linear, geometry-dominated regime of nano-pipettes is unexplored. Combining this biophysical correction with direct interrogation of vesicle priming states moves beyond phenomenological correction to mechanistically link recording artifacts to core synaptic physiology.

proposals.title: Liquid Junction Potentials as a Direct, Real-Time Reporter of Presynaptic Vesicle Fusion and Transmitter Release
proposals.motivation: Precise measurement of synaptic vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter release dynamics remains technically challenging. While capacitance measurements and pH-sensitive probes are used, they have limitations in temporal resolution or invasiveness. Liquid junction potentials (LJPs), arising from ionic diffusion gradients at pipette tips, are typically viewed as a nuisance to be corrected. We hypothesize that rapid, localized changes in extracellular ion concentration during vesicular release could directly modulate LJPs, offering a novel, label-free physiological signal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the exocytotic release of neurotransmitter from a synaptic vesicle generates a rapid, quantifiable shift in the local ionic composition within the synaptic cleft, which can be detected as a transient change in the liquid junction potential of a closely apposed recording pipette.
proposals.method: We will perform paired recordings from pre- and postsynaptic neurons in rodent cortical brain slices. The presynaptic pipette will be filled with a solution creating a defined LJP. Using ultra-high-resistance seals and a dedicated, custom-built amplifier circuit with a switched-frequency protocol to separate LJP shifts from membrane capacitance changes, we will monitor LJP dynamics during evoked and spontaneous release. This will be combined with established methods (pharmacology, Ca2+ uncaging, and mutant mouse models affecting priming and fusion) to correlate LJP transients with specific vesicle pool dynamics (e.g., fully-primed vs. super-primed vesicles) and short-term plasticity.
proposals.contribution: This work aims to establish LJP monitoring as a new, direct technique for studying presynaptic function with high temporal fidelity. It could provide unprecedented real-time readouts of vesicle fusion events, priming states, and release site clearance, directly testing sequential priming models. This would offer a complementary tool to existing methods like capacitance measurements or iGluSnFR imaging.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reconceptualizing the liquid junction potential from a static artifact to be subtracted into a dynamic, primary physiological signal reporting synaptic vesicle exocytosis, a fundamentally non-obvious application of a classical biophysical phenomenon.

proposals.title: Direct Experimental Test of Markovian Gating in Synaptic Vesicle Priming and Fusion
proposals.motivation: The kinetic models used to describe synaptic vesicle dynamics, such as the sequential two-step priming scheme, fundamentally assume Markovian (memoryless) state transitions.
